title: Add RUN priv'd test for build
body:
Podman 1.4.1 had problems with builds with a
RUN command that tried to to a privliged command.

This adds a gating test for that situation.

Signed-off-by: TomSweeneyRedHat <tsweeney@redhat.com>

comments.body:
/hold

Although it (mostly) lgtm, with a few minor nits, I cannot get the test to fail with a v1.4.1 binary (even though I can reproduce the failure by running the steps manually). I need to understand this.

Whew, this was a doozy.

I must sadly reject this PR. It is not useful for catching bz1721638, because that bz only manifests if stdout is a tty. The way BATS works, stdout (and stderr) are captured, so the condition does not trigger:
```
$ ./bin/bad-podman build testdir
[crashes as expected]

$ ./bin/bad-podman build testdir | cat
[succeeds]
```
I cannot right now think of any way to reproduce the crash in BATS. Can you?

@edsantiago I'd like it in still - future bugs may not be TTY dependent.

Though the TTY thing does explain exactly how this snuck past our CI everywhere, I think

Actually, I've got a better idea. Instead of two tests, can we just piggyback on the existing one?

* add `RUN apk add nginx` to the first dockerfile
* add the COMMIT check after `run_podman build`

Thanks!
@edsantiago great catch on the --tty thing.  I'm not sure how to get around that off hand, maybe I can toggle the tty switch?  I'll play.

I'll rework this test tomorrow pere @edsantiago 's comments.  Ed the only one I'd like to not take is the single test bit.  I think it's better to have multiple tests.  Longer term plan is to add many tests in here, and if an error condition is hit, I find it easier to parse where the error is if there are multiple tests.

I spent a big chunk of today trying to get around the stdout thing.  No luck, I'm going to have to wave the white flag on that one.  I will add the test to the baseline test though so at least it can be caught more easily.

> I think it's better to have multiple tests. 

I understand and will accept it if you choose that route; but before you commit, remember that these are not dainty little precise unit tests. These are catastrophe-catchers intended to test as much as possible in a reasonable amount of time. They are not expected to fail, but if they do they have good diagnostics that will display the exact commands run and exact errors seen, thus helping reproducibility. For these tests, it's good to overload as much as we can. Again, I will accept your decision either way.
Gotta confess, left it behind in my rear view mirror.  Will make the change per @edsantiago and will repush.  Thanks for the poke.
Changes are to a doc, a non-ci test and a ci test.  Test failures are outside of the changed ci test.  @mheon anyway to restart the test or should I repush?
@TomSweeneyRedHat, I restarted. You should be able to do that by clicking on the `Details` link in the job list.
Thanks @vrothberg, I looked but didn't see the button.  Could well have been a pre-tea issue.
I will let @edsantiago have final say on this one.
@edsantiago can you re-review and remove your hold is appropriate please?

@mheon @baude I'm not sure how to change the "waiting for status to be reported" bit on this PR?  Is there some bit tickling one of you can do to get this to merge?
